title: ASML mulls price raise for chipmaking tools despite TSM resistance
gist: ASML is planning to raise prices on its lithography tools, drawing pushback from its largest customer TSMC even as some Chinese chipmakers have already accepted higher DUV pricing; ASML's CFO cited a 'pretty strong runway' for Low-NA EUV price increases, with EUV systems priced $220M-$400M and DUV around $90M.

themes.theme: ASML pricing power versus TSMC pushback

insights.claim: ASML plans to raise prices for its chipmaking equipment, putting it in direct conflict with TSMC, its largest customer, which recently complained about ASML's high prices and is actively resisting the proposed increases.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: A pricing standoff between the sole EUV supplier and its biggest customer is a direct margin lever for ASML and a cost-pressure signal for TSMC's capex and, ultimately, wafer pricing to fabless customers.
insights.quote: TSM is already pushing back against ASML's pricing plans, the report added.

insights.claim: ASML CFO Roger Dassen said the company has 'a pretty strong runway for potential price improvements going forward' specifically for its Low-NA EUV machine, though long order lead times mean price increases would not affect near-term financials.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Direct management commentary confirming pricing-power intent on Low-NA EUV is a forward margin catalyst, though the lag from long lead times means the earnings impact is delayed rather than immediate.
insights.quote: a pretty strong runway for potential price improvements going forward

insights.claim: ASML's most advanced DUV systems sell for about $90 million apiece, last-generation EUV machines cost around $220 million, and the newest EUV models can reach up to $400 million, while TSMC has separately been reported holding back on buying ASML's newer high-NA EUV tools due to their higher cost.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Concrete tool-pricing anchors ($90M DUV, $220-400M EUV) are useful inputs for modeling ASML's average selling price mix and TSMC's incremental capex per unit of leading-edge capacity added.
insights.quote: ASML sells its most advanced DUV systems for about $90M apiece, while its last-generation EUV machines cost around $220M, and the newest EUV models can reach up to $400M.

themes.theme: China exposure to DUV price increases

insights.claim: Some Chinese chipmakers have already agreed to pay ASML's higher prices for DUV systems, even as TSMC resists price increases on both DUV and EUV equipment, and China is particularly exposed because DUV is among the most capable lithography tools it can still legally buy under U.S. export controls.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Chinese buyers' willingness to absorb DUV price hikes despite export-control constraints suggests inelastic demand there, while ASML expects about 20% of 2026 total net sales from China (14% of Q2's €6.6B system sales).
insights.quote: Some Chinese chipmakers have agreed to pay the higher prices for the DUV systems, the report added.

insights.claim: TSMC's heavy demand for its CoWoS advanced packaging technology, used to assemble AI accelerators designed by companies like Nvidia and Google, gives it an added incentive to secure more DUV lithography capacity even as it resists ASML's price increases.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Ties DUV tool demand directly to the CoWoS advanced-packaging bottleneck for AI accelerators, reinforcing that packaging capacity — not just leading-edge logic — is a binding constraint on AI chip supply.
insights.quote: DUV equipment is important to TSM's expansion of advanced packaging, another bottleneck in the AI supply chain.
